findings.evidence: reportServerError is referenced in only 6 of 55 api route files. report-server-error.ts's own header explains it exists precisely because a handler that CATCHES an error never bubbles to instrumentation.ts's onRequestError→PostHog wiring. scoring-tick's main per-item catch (lines 268-284) formats the error into the item's `error` column and calls failItem — but never calls reportServerError, so item-level pipeline failures (Claude parse errors, Exa failures, DB errors) are visible only in the admin jobs table, not in PostHog or via alert. The Stripe webhook's signature-verify catch (webhook/route.ts:20) silently returns 400 with no report.
findings.impact: Whole classes of prod failures (scoring items failing en masse, webhook signature mismatches after a secret rotation, find-email upstream errors) produce no alert and no aggregatable PostHog signal — you only find out by manually inspecting tables. Debugging a prod incident lacks centralized signal.
findings.recommendation: Call reportServerError in the scoring-tick per-item catch (with itemId/jobId context), in the webhook invalid-signature path, and standardize a thin wrapper so every /api route's top-level catch reports. Consider a structured logger (route, requestId, userId) instead of ad-hoc console.error so logs are queryable.

findings.title: Admin-alert dedupe is per-instance in-memory — alert storms still possible across serverless instances
findings.evidence: lastAlertAt is a module-level Map (line 22); the file's own comment (lines 13-16) notes 'The dedupe map is per-instance (serverless cold starts reset it).' On Fluid Compute with multiple concurrent instances, each instance has its own Map, so a fingerprint that fires across N instances emails up to N times per hour, and a burst that spawns many cold instances multiplies it further.
findings.impact: During a real incident (e.g. an enricher upstream down, throwing the same error across many concurrent scoring functions), the operator inbox can still be flooded despite the intended 1/hour cap — the opposite of what's needed mid-incident.
findings.recommendation: Move dedupe to the durable store the comment already suggests: a small Postgres table keyed on (fingerprint, last_sent_at) with an atomic conditional insert/update, so the 1/hour cap holds globally across instances.

findings.title: Cron secret compared with non-constant-time string equality
findings.evidence: return auth === `Bearer ${secret}` — a plain JS === comparison that short-circuits on the first differing byte, leaking timing information about CRON_SECRET. The same pattern is worth checking in src/lib/api-keys.ts (public-key auth).
findings.impact: Theoretically allows a timing side-channel to recover CRON_SECRET byte-by-byte. Low practical risk over the public internet (network jitter dominates), but it gates every cron that spends money (scoring, find-email) and is trivial to harden.
findings.recommendation: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length buffers (hash both sides to fixed length first to avoid length leakage). Audit src/lib/api-keys.ts for the same comparison.

findings.title: scoring-tick has no stale-claim reaper — a killed/timed-out function strands items in 'scoring' forever
findings.evidence: The item claim is atomic and idempotent (FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, lines 108-122, good). But the claim WHERE only re-claims status IN ('pending','resolved') (line 115). The per-item pipeline call (runEval/reEvaluate, lines 197-199) has up to 2 generateText attempts with NO abortSignal (eval-pipeline.ts:370). If the function is killed by maxDuration (300s) mid-item, that item stays in status 'scoring' with started_at set, and no later tick ever reclaims it — there is no reaper for stale 'scoring'/'resolving' rows.
findings.impact: An item whose function is killed gets stuck in 'scoring' forever: the parent job never reaches all-terminal, so the completion/credit-hold reconciliation (lines 288-333) never fires and the credit hold is never refunded. Exa+Claude spend for the partial attempt is incurred with no terminal state.
findings.recommendation: Add a stale-claim reaper at the top of each tick: reset items in 'scoring'/'resolving' with started_at older than a threshold back to 'pending' (or fail after K resets). Pass an abortSignal/timeout to generateText so a single item can't consume the whole maxDuration budget.

findings.title: refundCredits is not idempotent while topUpCredits is — asymmetry risks minting free credits on a double-refund
findings.evidence: topUpCredits gates on the credit_ledger payment_intent unique index (lines 93-104), so a retry is a no-op. refundCredits (line 64) has no such guard: it unconditionally increments the balance and inserts a 'refund' row (no unique key tying the refund to the originating score_debit). The reserve→run→refund flow depends on the caller invoking refundCredits exactly once on failure.
findings.impact: A retry or duplicate error path that calls refundCredits twice for the same failed eval would over-credit the user (mint free credits). Lower likelihood than the find-email charge bug because the score path is usually single-shot, but the asymmetry (topup idempotent, refund not) is a latent foot-gun.
findings.recommendation: Make refunds idempotent: key a refund ledger row to the originating score_debit ledgerId via a unique partial index and onConflictDoNothing, so a double-refund is a no-op. Audit all reserve callers to ensure exactly one refund on the failure path.

summary: The build foundation is fundamentally healthy: TypeScript strict mode is on with effectively zero escape hatches (0 ts-ignore, 3 `as any`, 8 scoped eslint-disables), cron timeouts are correctly budgeted, and secrets are properly gitignored. The main hygiene gaps are operational rather than structural: two divergent committed lockfiles (npm + pnpm) creating install-nondeterminism, ~30 production env vars (including Stripe and email-finder secrets) undocumented in .env.example, no CI pipeline so lint/test/build are never enforced pre-merge, and an unpinned Stripe API version.

findings.title: Two divergent lockfiles committed (package-lock.json AND pnpm-lock.yaml) with no packageManager pin
findings.evidence: Both `package-lock.json` (706 "resolved" entries, lockfileVersion 3) and `pnpm-lock.yaml` (668 resolutions) are tracked and committed together (commit 0c91a89). package.json has no `packageManager` and no `engines` field. The pre-commit hook shells out to `pnpm drizzle-kit generate`, implying pnpm is the intended local tool, yet a full npm lockfile is also maintained. The two lockfiles resolve a different number of packages, so they are already drifting.
findings.impact: Vercel and contributors pick an installer by lockfile heuristics. If Vercel installs from package-lock.json while devs run pnpm (or vice-versa), the deployed dependency graph differs from what was tested locally — the classic 'works on my machine' / phantom-version class of production bug. Drift will silently widen on every dependency change because only one lockfile gets updated per `add`.
findings.recommendation: Pick one package manager. Add `"packageManager": "pnpm@<version>"` to package.json, delete package-lock.json, and add it to .gitignore. Set Vercel's install command explicitly to `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`. Add an `engines.node` field to pin the runtime too.

findings.title: .env.example documents only ~16 of ~42 env vars actually read by the code; Stripe & other secrets undocumented
findings.evidence: Code reads ~42 distinct process.env keys (grep over src/ + scripts/) but .env.example lists only 16. Undocumented-but-required secrets include STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET, ANYMAILFINDER_API_KEY, LUMA_API_KEY, STACK_EXCHANGE_KEY, NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY/HOST, ADMIN_ALERT_EMAIL, plus all rate-limit knobs (API_*_PER_DAY_LIMIT, *_GLOBAL_PER_DAY) and feature flags (SCORING_CASCADE, SCORING_EFFORT, CASCADE_HAIKU_MIN/SONNET_MIN, ADMIN_CREDIT_ENFORCEMENT, CLAIM/DEV_API_WELCOME_EMAIL_ENABLED). Conversely APIFY_API_TOKEN is documented and IS used (nfx enricher), and CLERK_SECRET_KEY/NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY are documented and consumed by the @clerk SDK — those are fine.
findings.impact: A fresh deploy or new contributor cannot reproduce a working environment from .env.example. Billing (Stripe), email-finding, event sync (Luma), and analytics will silently fail or throw at first use (e.g. stripe.ts throws 'STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is not set'). Operators have no documented inventory of the tunable rate-limit / feature-flag surface.
findings.recommendation: Regenerate .env.example from a grep of process.env across src/ and scripts/, grouping into Required vs Optional and documenting defaults for each flag (e.g. SCORING_CASCADE behavior, CASCADE_*_MIN thresholds 95/85). Consider a small zod env-schema module that validates presence at boot for the required set.

findings.title: No CI pipeline — lint, typecheck, and tests are never enforced before merge
findings.evidence: There is no .github/workflows directory. The only automated gate is the husky pre-commit hook, which enforces PRD journaling and a drizzle schema-drift check but does NOT run `eslint`, `tsc`, `next build`, or `vitest`. The package has lint/test scripts (lines 9, 13) but nothing invokes them in CI. Vitest config and a tests/ dir exist, so tests are written but their passing status is unverified on push.
findings.impact: Type errors, lint violations, and test regressions can land on main and only surface in the Vercel production build (or worse, at runtime). The strict tsconfig and clean codebase are only protected by developer discipline; one bad merge from a less-careful session can break the deploy.
findings.recommendation: Add a GitHub Actions workflow (or Vercel-side check) running `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, `pnpm lint`, `tsc --noEmit`, and `pnpm test` on every PR; make it a required status check before merge to main.

findings.title: Stripe client instantiated with no pinned apiVersion
findings.evidence: `cached = new Stripe(key);` — no `{ apiVersion: "..." }` option is passed. The SDK falls back to the Stripe account's default API version, which Stripe can change out from under the integration (e.g. when the dashboard is upgraded), independent of code deploys.
findings.impact: Webhook payload shapes and API response fields can shift without a code change, silently breaking the webhook handler (src/app/api/stripe/webhook) or credit reconciliation. Hard to reproduce because the trigger is an account-level config change, not a deploy.
findings.recommendation: Pin explicitly: `new Stripe(key, { apiVersion: "2025-xx-xx" })` matching the version the webhook/billing code was written against, and bump deliberately when migrating.

findings.title: next.config.ts and the cron budget are clean — confirmed no build-time error suppression
findings.evidence: next.config.ts is empty (no `typescript.ignoreBuildErrors` / `eslint.ignoreDuringBuilds` escape hatches — good). tsconfig has `strict: true`. All long-running crons set explicit maxDuration: scoring-tick=300s with ITEMS_PER_TICK=5, find-email-tick=300s with BATCH=50/CONCURRENCY=10 (~30s wall-clock, well inside budget), refresh-mm=300s. vercel.json's 5 cron paths align with the documented jobs and use valid cron expressions. Note: tsconfig `exclude: ["scripts/**/*"]` means the ~20 maintenance scripts in scripts/ are NOT typechecked — minor, but worth awareness since they touch prod DBs.
findings.impact: Low. This is largely a positive/confirmation finding. The one latent gap is that scripts/ (including prod DB-mutating .mjs/.ts files) bypass strict typechecking and lint, so a bad migration script won't be caught by the build.
findings.recommendation: Optionally bring scripts/ under a separate tsconfig include or a lint pass given several of them write to the production Neon branch (per repo memory of past schema-drift outages). Otherwise no action needed on the cron/config side.
